title: Off-grid electrical systems: the basics
subtitle: Panels, charge controller, battery, and inverter, how the components connect
excerpt: An off-grid solar electrical system has four stages: solar panels generate DC; a charge controller regulates the charging of a battery bank; the battery stores it; and an inverter converts DC to AC for household loads. Sizing each stage requires knowing your daily kWh consumption and how many days of autonomy you need without generation.
sourceNotes: Solar Trade Association off-grid guidance. Victron Energy system sizing documentation. BS 7671 (IET Wiring Regulations) Appendix 17 for ELV and LV off-grid installations.

glossaryTerms.definition: Photovoltaic panels that convert sunlight to DC electricity. In an off-grid system, the DC output feeds a charge controller before entering the battery bank.
Kilowatt-hour. The unit for both energy generation (solar panels produce kWh per day) and energy storage (batteries are rated in kWh).
Kilowatt-peak. The rated output of solar PV panels under standard test conditions. Used to size the panel array.

content.text: system operates independently from the distribution network. Unlike a grid-tied system, surplus generation cannot be exported and shortfalls cannot be imported, the battery must cover the gap. This makes sizing critical: undersized batteries mean power cuts; oversized batteries mean wasted expenditure.
Solar panels: generate DC electricity during daylight. Sized in
Solar panels: generate DC electricity during daylight. Rated in
actual daily generation varies with weather and season.
Charge controller: regulates current from panels to battery. MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) controllers are more efficient than older PWM controllers. Critical for battery health and longevity.

Battery bank: stores energy for use when panels are not generating. Sized in kWh capacity. Lithium batteries (LFP chemistry) are the current standard for off-grid, longer cycle life and usable at full rated capacity vs lead-acid which should not be discharged below 50%.
Inverter: converts battery DC to AC for household loads. Sized by peak load (W) not average load.

) × days of autonomy ÷ depth of discharge. For a system with 2 kWh/day consumption, 3 days autonomy, and LFP batteries (DoD 90%): 2 × 3 ÷ 0.9 = 6.7 kWh battery capacity. In the UK, plan for 3-5 days of autonomy to handle consecutive cloudy winter days.

A 12V or 24V DC system (no inverter) is appropriate for outbuildings, cabins, or low-consumption setups (LED lighting, USB charging, 12V appliances). It avoids inverter losses (typically 5-15%) and inverter cost. A 230V AC system using an inverter is needed for standard UK appliances (fridges, washing machines, laptops, power tools). An inverter/charger combination unit handles both the inversion and a backup generator or grid top-up connection in a single unit.

attrs.title: Electrical work
attrs.body: Connecting any AC wiring to an inverter or to household circuits is electrical installation work covered by BS 7671. It requires either a qualified electrician or a suitably qualified DIYer working within permitted self-notification rules. DC wiring below 50V is not covered by Part P but battery banks store large amounts of energy, short circuits can cause fires. Fuse every DC circuit close to the battery.
